#25 Hiking to Kongo Falls in Tokyo

March 27, 2024 1 hour 35 minutes

All transcripts are machine generated.

Transcript

And at that point, I was halfway. So I guess I played a trick on myself. But yeah, this thing is pretty rough. And there was one part where instead of being safely on the top of the ridge, it looped sort of around the side of a ridge. And that's the only part where it was like, you know, it was fine. It was still a flat path. It was no big deal. But I did have to kind of be like, okay, don't overthink this because if you did slip for some reason, what if this was the time that a spider fell on the back of your neck and you went, what? If you fell down this thing, you'd be really, really hurt. Yeah. Where, like, I mean, like right where I am now. Like, yeah, if I fell down that side, I'd be hurt. If I fell down that side, I'd be hurt. But it just, I don't know how I would, even if I fell, I'd have to roll 12 feet. And then I'd start really falling. Like, it's just, it's really no big deal. But that one part, I guess that's the thing with these trails. Like, this might explain why there was the, hey, idiot, don't, don't go on this trail sign. Is that it doesn't matter if 99% of the trail is pretty much fine. If the 1% say, you know, like that part on the ridge and the very final descent down to the highway, if those parts are legitimately dangerous, which I would say they are, I guess that is the rating for the whole trail, right? I don't know how these things work or how people delineate these things, but that makes sense to me. The most dangerous part is how dangerous the trail is. So even though this trail is mostly no biggie, and the fact that it's so natural is actually nice. I mean, instead of steps that people made, which is nice in its own way, but like just there, I just walked up routes. Just routes that trees have made some natural steps. So this is one of those things that I'm glad I stumbled into it. This is a neat thing to do. But I won't do it again, and I wouldn't recommend anyone else do it either.

It is a little too dangerous. Although, I mean, I did just pass, there was a building I saw yesterday with a really tall spire kind of, and I was like, I wonder what that building is. I still don't know, but I passed by it. It was right there. But right there, quote-unquote, you know, it was right there down a precipitous drop where I would absolutely break both my arms. So I mean, I'm near civilization, and I'm not. I do feel like this is, there's going to be times in Fukuoka if I do what I did last time, that are going to be a little similar to this, where I may be a little bit slightly ill-advisably off the beaten path. I've learned how good this GPS is, like I'm amazed I even found the entrance to this path, because it literally was just scramble marks on the side of the mountain. It didn't look like a path at that point at all. I would have just walked right by, but this thing is so accurate that I walked past the first entrance, and I only walked an extra 10 feet, but that was enough for my blue dot to be like your past the point you've gone past, and I was, and it's just interesting, I guess I knew that from the city and stuff, like it always told me exactly what corner I was on or whatever. It's not like it was just, here's sort of where you are, but to be kind of out in the middle of nowhere in such an abstract, you know, I'm just looking at a little dotted line on a road, and then in real life, a barely noticeable scramble path up the side of a fucking cliff, and it was all accurate. That's really cool. This phone is amazing. I think I probably mentioned at some point, but how I got this is it said it had shadow on eBay, and I was like, what does shadow mean? And reminded me of this story of this guy who one of these dudes who just got like abandoned in a cabin way up north somewhere. I don't really remember the details, but he thought he had enough supplies for the winter. He did not. He was just slowly starving to death. He had a journal that he kept, you know, there's all these YouTube channels that deal in such stories. But instead this guy was like, oh, good, I saw the guys. He just sort of waved at them real quick. Like, yay. And immediately went into his cabin and started getting his stuff together. So to the people flying overhead, they saw him, but they were like, okay, I guess he's just waving hello, like he did not signify in any way that he was in danger. He could have at the very least just continued to stand there and wave his arms back and forth, really make it clear that he was in a situation. So he just immediately, he just assumed that they immediately assumed the same that they just understood where they, on the other hand, were like, well, he wouldn't be out here if he didn't have the right stuff if he wasn't prepared. It doesn't matter what's in the cave, it's the thrill, the excitement, the danger, the fear of like going in the cave. That was the whole thing.
